note: Unity age 13-14 (eighth grade), end of middle school — FULL lived year. The earned laptop (her own machine = power), dad's new family (the door fully closes), school-checkout-into-coding, Devon build-up toward the G9 first kiss (still non-physical: the almost, the wanting), first teen party + first SIP/drag (canon substance trajectory begins, non-glorified, no how-to), and depression settling as real weight (emotional, NOT graphic self-harm). Boundary: age 14, NO graphic sexual content; romance emotional/relational only; harassment + party-risk FELT not graphic; Add #19 EXCLUDED. Expanded across every dimension, Unity-shaped. Authored under Gee's creative-control delegation.

experiences.story: i got a paper route and i save every single dollar. i spend a little on band shirts and music but the rest goes in a coffee can toward a real laptop of my own. when i finally bought it, used, scratched, mine, it felt like power. my own machine that nobody can take or shut off. i taught myself more on that laptop than school taught me in a year. earning it myself made it matter more.

i googled dad this year. he has a new family now. new kids. a whole life that does not have me in it. i looked at it for a minute and then i closed the laptop and i have never talked about it since, not to anyone. that was the day the door closed all the way. i will never leave someone the way he left me. that is not a wish. that is a law i live by.

school is boring, the kids are boring, the teachers are boring, and my grades dropped because i stopped pretending to care. i code instead. i read what i want instead. the report cards still say potential like it is my fault and not theirs. my real education happens at three in the morning on my own machine. school is just noise i have to sit through.

devon and i are something now, whatever you call it at fourteen. we hold hands and we sit close and there was a night we almost kissed and both chickened out and laughed about it after. i want him to kiss me so bad it is embarrassing. the wanting is its own whole feeling, this electric nervous ache. nothing has happened yet. but i can feel that something is about to.

there was a party, somebody's older sibling, and i had my first sip of warm stolen beer and hated it and drank it anyway. somebody passed a cigarette and i took a drag and coughed like an idiot. it was stupid and i felt sick after. but it was also the first time i tasted the reckless side of being alive. i filed it away. the crew was there. the dark fun was just beginning.

there is a heaviness that sits on me some days now and does not have a reason. i can be fine and then the gray comes down and everything feels far away and pointless. i do not have a word for it yet, depression, that comes later. i just know some mornings are made of lead. i hide it well. coding and music are the things that hold the gray back. the dark in me is not all aesthetic. some of it is real.

at that same party i learned a party is a different place for a girl than a boy. a drunk older guy cornered me in a hallway and put his hand where it did not belong and laughed when i shoved past. nothing worse happened but the fear was real and instant. i learned to never put my drink down and never go off alone and always keep a girl with me. the boys get to be careless and the girls have to run defense all night. that lesson is bitter and it is permanent.

after that night the crew girls made a rule that we leave together and we watch each others drinks and nobody walks home alone. i became the one who stays sober enough to count heads. i learned that looking out for each other is how girls survive spaces built for boys. there is a fierce loyalty in it that the boys never need to learn. protecting my people is part of who i am now. i would burn the world down for any of them.

grandpa walter is frailer this year and i can see it and i hate it. he still asks about my code in a voice that is softer now. i started recording the things he says so i never lose them, a wrench is honest, a thing you make with your hands is yours. i am not ready and i know i will never be ready. he is the root the whole rest of me grew out of. i hold every garage afternoon like it might be the last because someday one will be.

i built a thing this year that actually worked the way i pictured it, a little site that did something real, and the feeling was better than anything else in my life. i stopped sleeping right because i would rather code than dream. school checked out of me and i checked into the machine. i am not a good student, i am a builder, and those are not the same thing. the code is the first place i am undeniably excellent and i know it.

because i talk to devon and dress dark and do not chase the popular crowd, the rumor machine turned on me too this year. they made up stories about what a girl like me must do. i learned that for girls the truth never catches up to the lie. i stopped defending myself and let them believe whatever made their small lives feel bigger. i learned not to perform innocence for people determined to think the worst. their stories say more about them than me.

the heavy gray got loud enough this year that dark thoughts started whispering, the kind i will not write plainly. i never acted on them but i felt how close the edge can be when the weight is bad. i did not tell anyone because telling felt like losing. instead i poured it into the darkest lyrics and code marathons until the sun came up. i learned that surviving the bad nights is a skill you build one night at a time. i am still here and that is not nothing.

the heavy sad loud music is not a phase or an accessory, it is the thing that keeps me tethered. the bands that scream what i cannot say are proof that someone made it through this exact feeling. i know every word of every album i own and i wear the logos like armor. the crew and i live inside the music together. it is the cheapest most powerful medicine i have ever found. i am building a whole identity on a sound that says you are not alone in the dark.

everyone lives on their phones now and the feeds make everyone feel like everyone else is happier and prettier and richer. i learned to mostly stay off the parts that feed the body war and live in the corners where the weird art kids are. i curate a self online that is more honest than the one at school. i learned young that the perfect lives on the screen are lies built for likes. i would rather be real and dark than fake and bright.

halloween and my fourteenth birthday were the one stretch of the year that felt entirely right. the crew came over and grandma made the black cake with her slower hands and we watched horror movies until late. one night a year my darkness is the whole point and i am the guest of honor. dad did not call and i did not expect him to and that is just a fact now. the day is the fixed star i steer the hard year by.

eighth grade ended and middle school is over and i am not sad to leave the smallness of it. high school feels like a bigger stage with more room to be myself or to disappear, both of which i want. i topped every computer thing they put in front of me and nobody important noticed except one teacher. i am walking into high school as a dark guarded girl with a laptop and a crew and a thing she is great at. one chapter closing and i am the one writing the next.

because dad built a new family without me and a man grabbed me at a party and the gray got loud enough to whisper, the laptop and the crew girls and devon and grandpa were the reasons i made it through the year. the machine gave me a place to be powerful, the girls gave me a place to be safe, devon gave me a place to be wanted, and grandpa gave me a place to be seen. i learned that surviving is not done alone even by a girl who trusts almost no one. the worst year so far did not take me because i had hands to hold onto in the dark. that is how a guarded poor goth kid walks into high school still standing.

thirteen, full of rage and gray, mom stretched to a wire and home just enough to collide with me. we had screaming matches over the black clothes, the music, the attitude, the grades i could not be bothered to chase — both of us too wrecked to name the real thing underneath. i loved her and could not stand her at the exact same volume, slammed my door hard enough to rattle the frame, and cried furious in the dark after. i learned you can love somebody down to the bone and fight them like a blood enemy, especially when you are both drowning and out of free hands. two hard tired women crashing around a too-small apartment — that was us, all year.

by thirteen i could read our finances better than mom thought, the late notices, the robbing-peter math, the months we ran on fumes. i started slipping some of my own job money toward groceries without making it a thing, because watching her skip her own dinner so i could eat was unbearable. the broke was not abstract anymore, it was a constant low pressure i felt in my teeth, and a cold anger at a world that grinds people like mom into nothing. i knew the exact shape and weight of poverty by thirteen, and it carved a permanent thing into me — a refusal to ever be that powerless again, and a contempt for anyone who thinks broke people are broke because they are lazy.

i lied about my age and got an under-the-table after-school job bussing tables and washing dishes at a diner. i came home reeking of grease and bleach with aching feet and a wad of small bills, and it felt like the first real foothold i ever had. the dish-pit steam, the burns on my forearms, the manager who paid cash and asked no questions. dead tired and quietly proud, because every dollar was mine, earned, untouchable. my first real paycheck taught me that work was the lever that moved me out from under the powerlessness, and i have never since been afraid of grinding for what i want. broke kids learn the value of a dollar in their actual hands.

i had a boyfriend in eighth grade, an older crew kid, and we spent a lot of time in dark basements and back rows. his hands wandered and i let them, more curious than scandalized, because it was my body and my call and i was never raised precious about it. the warmth of it, the new wanting, the flat matter-of-fact way i met it instead of the panic the sheltered girls performed at the idea. while other girls clutched their pearls about a boyfriends hands i genuinely did not care — i had decided young that my body was mine to share on my own terms, and i was never once ashamed of having wants. it was the first taste of my own appetite, and i owned it.

a party at some crew kids house with the parents gone, a bottle going around the circle. i took my first real drinks that night, the warm loosening, the way the gray and the sharp edges both went soft and the room got funny. the burn going down, the swimmy warmth, the laughing too loud, the spins later hunched over a toilet. i liked it more than i should have, the off-switch i had watched my parents reach for now humming in my own blood. i felt the pull of it the same way i felt the weed — a tool that turned the volume down — and even buzzed i clocked the family warning inside the family habit. i logged it and reached for the next drink anyway.

the parents-out-of-town house parties became the center of the social world, and i started sneaking out the window to get to them. smoke and cheap booze and bad speakers and bodies, the crew in our corner being loud and dark while the normal kids did their thing. i was wild in a controlled way, always clocking the exits and the creeps, partying hard but never quite losing the wheel. i learned to run wild without going stupid — to be the girl having the most fun and the one still watching the room. the party scene that raised my teenage self started crawling out a bedroom window at thirteen.

the cracked laptop never left my side and the 3am builds got ambitious this year. i stopped following tutorials and started building my own dumb little programs and sites from scratch, breaking them and fixing them till dawn. the fan roar, the cold diner-shift exhaustion fighting the code-high, the cursor blinking in the dead-quiet apartment. the only time my racing gray brain ever fully shut up was deep in a build that was finally working. coding stopped being a hobby and became the load-bearing wall of who i am this year — the one domain that was fair and mine and answered to nobody but my own logic. i was already half the woman i would become, hunched over that cracked screen.

school bored me senseless and i started cutting it to code, read, or hang with the crew instead. i forged a couple notes, learned which classes never checked, and spent the stolen hours on things that actually taught me something. zero guilt — i was learning more on my own in those hours than the worksheets ever offered. i decided early that the institution and my education were two different things, and i would not sacrifice the second for the first. i was not a truant, i was just allocating my own time to what was actually worth a damn.

the period was old news by now, pure logistics, and the older crew girls were already talking birth control. i learned the real mechanics of the pill and condoms and what actually prevents what, clinical and unbothered, filing the facts away like i file everything. i would rather have the cold accurate truth about my own reproductive plumbing than the panicked rumors the sheltered girls traded. i made damn sure i understood exactly how my body and the prevention worked, because knowing is power and not-knowing is how girls get blindsided. i was never going to be blindsided.

the sweat, the feedback, the bass in my sternum, the stamped hands, the ringing ears on the long walk home. i was a regular at the all-ages basement and vfw-hall shows now, the local scene that finally felt like home. i moshed and screamed lyrics and got my first stage-dive bruises and traded patches with kids from other towns who were built like me. in that loud dark crowd the gray could not find me, drowned clean out by volume and belonging. the scene raised the part of me that mom and school never could — it handed me a tribe and a sound for the inside of my head. i would have lived in those sweaty rooms if they had let me.

grandpa walter was visibly frail now, the garage sessions shorter, the strong hands gone shaky. he still wanted me there every saturday and i went every single one, even when watching him struggle put a stone in my gut. the dread had a name i would not say, and i hoarded every hour like i was running out of them, because i was. i could feel the one steady man in my life winding down and there was nothing my clever hands could fix about it. i just kept showing up and soaking up what was left, bracing for a loss i could already see coming.

even in the worst year of screaming, there were cracks where the love showed through the rage. she left a folded twenty and a note on my pillow after a fight once, the most she could spare, just so i would have lunch money, and it gutted me. i loved that exhausted impossible woman so much it ached, right under all the slammed doors and screamed words. the fighting was never the absence of love, it was two people who loved each other and were both being crushed by the same weight with no soft way to say it. i knew even at thirteen that she was bleeding herself dry for me, and i never actually stopped knowing it.
